results.possibleIdentity: Bernard Childs (1910–1985) was an American painter, printmaker, and sculptor who was a pioneer in integrating industrial technology and tools into the fine arts. Born in Brooklyn to Russian immigrant parents, he spent much of his career working between Paris and New York, where he was a long-time resident of the Hotel Chelsea. He is best known for his innovation of the 'power drypoint' technique, in which he used electric power tools, such as high-speed dentist drills and rotary burrs, to directly engrave metal printing plates—a method that bypassed traditional chemical etching. In the late 1960s and early 1970s, his work expanded into three-dimensional 'illuminated acrylic light sculptures.' These involved engraved sheets of acrylic or Plexiglas that were lit from the edges or below to make the incised lines glow, a series that was prominently showcased in a 1969 retrospective at the Storm King Art Center. His interest in the intersection of art and science was further highlighted by his 1962 visit to a nuclear cyclotron, which inspired his 'Holocaust' series of prints. While he is often associated with the technological art movement of the era due to his use of industrial machinery and light-based media, he is distinct from the choreographer Lucinda Childs, who was a founding participant in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) group's '9 Evenings: Theatre & Engineering.' However, Bernard Childs' experimental use of light and industrial tools closely aligned with the mission of E.A.T., and he was an active figure in the same New York avant-garde circles during the late 1960s and 1970s.
